steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Martinez and colleagues concluded that H. ochraceiceps links T. caesius alarm calls specifically to danger. To support that conclusion, we need evidence that rules out the rival explanation: that the birds react to any sound, not just alarm calls.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘When Martinez and colleagues played control sounds of random noise in the vicinity of H. ochraceiceps, the birds displayed no reaction.’: Shows the birds do NOT react to arbitrary sound — only to the alarm call. This isolates the alarm call as the meaningful trigger and directly supports the danger-association conclusion. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘Other bird species than H. ochraceiceps also showed a tendency to freeze or scatter when alarm calls were played.’: About other species, not about whether H. ochraceiceps specifically links the call to danger. Irrelevant. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Martinez and colleagues played alarm calls from different T. caesius individuals and observed no significant variation in the responses.’: Shows consistency across individuals, but doesn't address whether the birds tie the calls to danger versus reacting to any sound. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘In some instances, H. ochraceiceps froze or scattered when Martinez and colleagues approached but before they began playing sounds.’: Undermines the conclusion — the birds reacted without any alarm call at all, suggesting something other than the call triggered the response. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
